meta.title: The role of malic enzyme in the regulation of lipid accumulation in filamentous fungi

abstractHtml: The hypothesis is advanced that NADP(+)-malic enzyme (ME; EC 1.1.1.40) is an important activity in regulating the extent of lipid accumulation in filamentous fungi. In Mucor circinelloides, a fungus capable of accumulating only 25% (w/w, dry wt) lipid, even under the most propitious conditions, ME disappears 15--20 h after nitrogen exhaustion, coincident with the cessation of lipid accumulation. In contrast, ME in Mortierella alpina, a fungus capable of accumulating 50% (w/w, dry wt) lipid, remains active for over 60 h after N-exhaustion during which time lipid accumulation continues. No other enzyme activity studied, including the lipogenic enzymes acetyl-CoA carboxylase, fatty acid synthase, diacyglycerol acyltransferase, ATP:citrate lyase and the NADPH-generating enzymes glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase, 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase and NADP(+):isocitrate dehydrogenase, demonstrated any correlation with the accumulation of storage lipid in either fungus. Full activity of ME is restored in Mr. circinelloides within 4 h by adding NH(+)(4) to the cultures, but this is prevented by adding cycloheximide as an inhibitor of protein synthesis. This suggests that the decrease in ME activity occurs due to down-regulation of the ME gene.
